svg.text: THE INDEPENDENT GUIDE TO IBM-COMPATIBLE PERSONAL COMPUTERS
• polyDBackup: Enterprise Database Backup Done Right
• sheLLaMa: Your Shell Gets an AI Brain
• Quick Fix: lsb_release Returns from the Dead

g.text: ,migrate my PC to the new machine

svg.text: The personal computer that runs both CP/M and MS-DOS.

g.text: Volume in drive A is RAINBOW

text.tspan: The DEC Rainbow 100+ gives you the best of both worlds. Run your existing CP/M-80
software on the built-in Z80 processor — then switch to MS-DOS for the latest
business applications. 256KB RAM standard, expandable to 896KB. Dual 400KB floppy
drives. Optional 10MB Winchester hard disk. Full networking with DECnet.
PROCESSOR:
8088 (MS-DOS) + Z80 (CP/M)
MEMORY:
256KB–896KB RAM
DISPLAY:
12" green phosphor, 80×24
STORAGE:
Dual 400KB 5¼" floppy
HARD DISK:
Optional 10MB Winchester
NETWORK:
DECnet Ethernet optional
PORTS:
2 serial, 1 parallel, 1 modem
PRICE:
From $2,595

svg.text: Circle number 1 on the reader service card.

text.tspan: M
oving to a new Windows PC has always meant a lost weekend. PCmigrate, a free utility from PFWorks, fixes that.
One click exports your entire environment — installed software, license keys, winget packages, WSL distributions
(as .tar or .vhdx), and even AppData folders — to an external drive. On the new machine, run the generated restore
script and watch 'winget import' and 'wsl --import' bring everything back. Our test: 47 apps, 2 WSL distros, under
5 minutes to export, 11 minutes to restore. The dark-themed WPF GUI is polished and responsive. WSL-Only mode
makes it equally useful as a standalone backup tool for developers who live in Linux subsystems. The interactive
HTML checklist with per-app download links and hidden license keys is a thoughtful touch. SmartScreen warnings
on the unsigned installer are the only real friction. Highly recommended.

svg.text: Circle number 203 on the reader service card.

text.tspan: D
atabase backups are like insurance: boring until you need them. polyDBackup makes them less boring and far
more reliable. This shell-based tool dumps PostgreSQL, MySQL, or MariaDB to S3 with compression, optional
AES-256 encryption, and — here's the clever bit — automated restore verification via table-level checksums.
The safety model is impressive. A hard-coded staging database name prevents accidental writes to production.
The backup user gets read-only grants on production tables and full control only on the staging workspace.
Even a misconfigured cron job can't damage live data. Retention management purges old backups from S3
automatically. Runs as a container, a systemd timer, or a plain cron job.
We tested with a 40 GB PostgreSQL instance: dump, compress, encrypt, upload, and verify completed in
under 12 minutes. The '.verified' marker in S3 after a successful checksum comparison gives peace of mind
that no other backup tool provides out of the box. Deducting one star only because documentation assumes
familiarity with S3 CLI configuration. Otherwise excellent.

svg.text: Circle number 204 on the reader service card.

Circle number 92 on the reader service card.

text.tspan: Supports:
PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB
Storage:
Any S3-compatible (AWS, MinIO, Wasabi, etc.)
Features:
Compression, AES-256 encryption, retention mgmt, restore verification via checksums
Deploy:
Docker container, systemd timer, or cron
Requires:
Bash, S3 CLI (aws/mc), database client tools
Price:
Free (GPL-3.0)
W
hat if your shell could write its own commands? sheLLaMa integrates
large language models directly into bash and PowerShell, creating what
the developer calls an "agentic shell." Type a natural-language request
prefixed with a comma, and the AI proposes commands, executes them
(with your approval), reads the output, and iterates up to 10 rounds.
The tool supports local LLMs via Ollama, cloud fallback, distributed
backends for load balancing, and runs fully offline after the initial model
pull. Permission tiers ensure destructive commands are blocked while
read-only operations auto-execute. It even snapshots your bash environ-
ment (functions, aliases, variables) so the AI's commands inherit context.
Beyond the shell, sheLLaMa offers a web GUI, REST API, OpenAI-
compatible endpoint (works with Cursor and Continue), image generation
via Stable Diffusion, and even a cloud cost estimation dashboard. It's
ambitious — perhaps too ambitious — but impressively cohesive.
We tested with Ollama running Llama 3 on an RTX 4090. Response
times averaged 1.2 seconds for simple commands, 4-6 seconds for
multi-step agentic loops. The conversation save/load and branching
features are genuinely useful for long exploratory sessions. Auto-
compaction keeps context within model limits gracefully.
The PowerShell integration is equally capable — rare for AI coding
tools that treat Windows as an afterthought. File editing uses diff-
based search-and-replace with previews before confirming, and web
search lets the AI look up documentation mid-task.
The admin console provides real-time stats on token usage, costs,
and backend health. If you run multiple Ollama instances, the load
balancer distributes work intelligently. Half a star deducted only
because initial setup (model pulling, backend config) requires non-
trivial Linux knowledge. Once running, it's superb.

svg.text: Circle number 205 on the reader service card.

text.tspan: Red Hat dropped the 'lsb_release' command from RHEL 9 and its
derivatives, breaking countless scripts that depend on it. This tiny shell
script drop-in replacement parses /etc/os-release and provides identical
output. Install it, and your existing automation works again. Dead simple,
exactly as it should be. No dependencies beyond bash.

svg.text: Circle number 206 on the reader service card.

text.tspan: Testing DHCP on bridged or VLAN-tagged interfaces is a pain. This
script takes a MAC address and bridge name, fires a DHCP request on
that specific interface, and shows you exactly what happened. The TUI
mode (--tui flag) provides a split-screen display with real-time packet
capture. Invaluable for libvirt/KVM and containerized network debugging.

svg.text: Circle number 207 on the reader service card.

g.text: Works in any IBM PC, XT, AT, or compatible. 10Base-2 (BNC) and AUI connectors.
On-board 8KB buffer. Hardware-configurable IRQ and I/O address. Novell-certified.
Includes drivers for NetWare, NDIS, and Packet Driver. $295 suggested retail.
Circle number 34 on the reader service card.

text.tspan: After reading your preview of PCmigrate (April issue), I immediately set it up for WSL-only backups. Last week I
accidentally ran 'rm -rf /' inside my Ubuntu 22.04 instance instead of the container I was targeting. PCmigrate had
a 3-hour-old VHDX export on my USB drive. Restored in two minutes. Subscription to Personal Computing: $47/year.
Not losing six months of ML training configs: priceless. — D. Markham, Portland, OR

svg.text: polyDBackup CHECKSUM VERIFICATION CAUGHT A SILENT CORRUPTION

text.tspan: We've been running polyDBackup for three months against our production PostgreSQL cluster. Last Tuesday the
verification step flagged a checksum mismatch on our orders table. Turns out a bad RAM stick was flipping bits
during dumps. Without the checksums, we'd have been restoring corrupted backups and never known. The staging
database safety model also gives our DBA peace of mind — no chance of accidental production writes.
— R. Vasquez, Systems Architect, Miami, FL
I was using GitHub Copilot CLI, a separate Ollama wrapper, and ChatGPT in a browser tab. sheLLaMa replaced all
three. The agentic loop that proposes, executes, reads output, and iterates is genuinely useful for complex sysadmin
tasks. The permission system prevents disasters, and conversation branching lets me explore "what if" scenarios
without losing my main thread. Plus it runs fully local on my workstation. No data leaves my network.
— K. Johansson, DevOps Engineer, Stockholm, Sweden
